subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalaamu alaykum, and welcome to Unit 3. In this unit you will learn how scientists turn the world into numbers. Science is not only about watching and describing; it is about measuring carefully and then calculating to discover things you cannot see just by looking. In this unit you will master two of the most useful calculations in all of physics: density, which tells you how tightly packed the matter in an object is, and speed, which tells you how fast something is moving. Both are worked out from simple measurements you can take at home with a jug, a ruler, a kitchen scale and a clock.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Science 0893 — Stage 8 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the things below. Come back to this list at the end and check yourself honestly.
Explain what density means as the amount of mass packed into a given volume, and state its units (g/cm3 or kg/m3).
Measure mass and volume correctly, including finding the volume of an irregular object by displacement of water.
Calculate density using the relationship density = mass / volume, showing every step of your working.
Rearrange the density formula to find mass or volume when the other quantities are known.
Explain floating and sinking by comparing the density of an object with the density of the liquid it is placed in.
Explain what speed means as the distance travelled in a given time, and state its units (m/s or km/h).
Calculate speed using the relationship speed = distance / time, showing every step of your working.
Rearrange the speed formula to find distance or time when the other quantities are known.
Read and draw distance-time graphs, and describe motion (fast, slow, or stopped) from the shape of the line.

concepts.title: Measuring and Calculating — part 1
concepts.explanation: Assalaamu alaykum, and welcome to Unit 3. In this unit you will learn how scientists turn the world into numbers. Science is not only about watching and describing; it is about measuring carefully and then calculating to discover things you cannot see just by looking. In this unit you will master two of the most useful calculations in all of physics: density, which tells you how tightly packed the matter in an object is, and speed, which tells you how fast something is moving. Both are worked out from simple…

concepts.title: Measuring and Calculating — part 2
concepts.explanation: Think about the questions this unit will let you answer. Why does a heavy wooden dhow float on the sea at Berbera while a small stone dropped from your hand sinks straight to the bottom? Why does cooking oil sit in a golden layer on top of water instead of mixing in? How fast does a camel really walk compared with a car driving from Hargeisa to Berbera? If a bus leaves Mogadishu and you know the distance and the time, can you work out its speed without ever seeing the speedometer? By the end of this unit, you will…
concepts.example: This unit is also about something quieter but just as important: doing measurement well. A measurement is only useful if it is honest and careful. You will learn to choose the right unit, to read a scale properly, to…

concepts.title: Measuring and Calculating — part 3
concepts.explanation: This unit is also about something quieter but just as important: doing measurement well. A measurement is only useful if it is honest and careful. You will learn to choose the right unit, to read a scale properly, to repeat a reading so you can trust it, and to notice when a number looks wrong. These habits of careful, honest measuring are the foundation of every science that comes after, from chemistry to medicine to engineering. They are also a quiet form of good character: to weigh and measure truthfully,…
concepts.example: You are learning this at home, at your own pace, with your AI tutor ready to help you at any hour, and with your teacher meeting you about twice a week. That means you are in charge of your own understanding. This is a…

concepts.title: Measuring and Calculating — part 4
concepts.explanation: You are learning this at home, at your own pace, with your AI tutor ready to help you at any hour, and with your teacher meeting you about twice a week. That means you are in charge of your own understanding. This is a calculation-heavy unit, so keep a pencil and a notebook beside you at all times. Do not just read the worked examples; copy them out, cover the answer, and try each one yourself. A formula only becomes yours when your own hand has used it. Let us begin.
concepts.example: [Fact] Did You Know? A block of iron and a block of cork can be exactly the same size, yet the iron is far heavier. The iron is not made of 'more space', it is made of matter that is packed much more tightly. That…

concepts.title: Measuring and Calculating — part 5
concepts.explanation: [Fact] Did You Know? A block of iron and a block of cork can be exactly the same size, yet the iron is far heavier. The iron is not made of 'more space', it is made of matter that is packed much more tightly. That tightness of packing is what scientists call density, and it is the reason a tiny iron nail sinks while a huge wooden log floats.
concepts.example: By the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the things below. Come back to this list at the end and check yourself honestly.

explorations.title: Measuring the Density of Household Objects
explorations.context: To measure the mass and volume of several small objects and calculate the density of each, then compare them.
explorations.prompt: Before you begin, write down what you think: Which of your objects will have the greatest density, and which the least? Will the biggest object always be the densest? Predict, then test.
explorations.answer: Which object had the greatest density? Which had the least? Was the biggest object also the densest? How did you find the volume of an object that was not a neat box shape? Why does this displacement method work? All your objects sank. What does that tell you about their densities compared with the…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To measure the mass and volume of several small objects and calculate the density of each, then compare them.

explorations.title: The Float and Sink Test (Density Column)
explorations.context: To observe how liquids and objects of different densities arrange themselves, and to connect floating and sinking to density.
explorations.prompt: Predict: If you pour cooking oil and water into the same glass, which will float on top? If you drop in a grape and a piece of cork, where will each one rest? Write your predictions before testing.
explorations.answer: Which liquid floated on top, oil or water? Using density, explain why (recall oil is about 0.92 g/cm3 and water is 1 g/cm3). Any object that floated on the water but sank through the oil has a density between which two values? Why did the stone sink all the way to the bottom while the cork floated…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe how liquids and objects of different densities arrange themselves, and to connect floating and sinking to density.

explorations.title: Measuring the Speed of a Runner
explorations.context: To measure a distance and a time, and use them to calculate the speed of a person running or walking.
explorations.prompt: Predict: How many metres per second do you think you can run? Will you be faster running or walking? By how much? Write your predictions before testing.
explorations.answer: What was your running speed in metres per second? What was your walking speed? How many times faster was running? Why did you time the run three times and take the average instead of trusting one single time? If one of your three times was very different from the other two, what should you do with…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To measure a distance and a time, and use them to calculate the speed of a person running or walking.

explorations.title: The Rolling Ball and the Distance-Time Graph
explorations.context: To measure how far a rolling ball travels over equal time intervals, and to draw a distance-time graph of its motion.
explorations.prompt: Predict: As a ball rolls along the ground and slows down, will it cover the same distance in each second, or less and less? What will the line on the graph look like?
explorations.answer: Did the ball cover the same distance in each second, or less each time as it slowed down? Look at your graph. Is the line straight or does it curve? Where is the line steepest, and what does that tell you about the ball's speed there? Choose one second of the journey. Using the distance covered in…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To measure how far a rolling ball travels over equal time intervals, and to draw a distance-time graph of its motion.

visualModels.title: Measuring and Calculating — part 6
visualModels.purpose: By the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the things below. Come back to this list at the end and check yourself honestly.

methods.title: Measuring the Density of Household Objects
methods.example: To measure the mass and volume of several small objects and calculate the density of each, then compare them.
steps: Weigh the first object on the kitchen scale and write down its mass in grams.
Pour water into the measuring jug to a level you can read clearly, for example 200 cm3. Record this starting level.
Gently lower the object into the water until it is fully covered. Record the new, higher water level.
Work out the volume of the object by subtracting the starting level from the new level.
Calculate the density using D = mass / volume. Show your working with the four steps.

methods.title: The Float and Sink Test (Density Column)
methods.example: To observe how liquids and objects of different densities arrange themselves, and to connect floating and sinking to density.
steps: Half fill the glass with water and let it settle.
Gently pour a layer of cooking oil on top of the water. Watch what happens and record which liquid ends up on top.
One at a time, gently drop in each test object and record where it comes to rest: floating on the oil, floating between oil and water, or sinking to the bottom.
Draw a simple labelled picture of the finished layers and where each object settled.
Record your observations in the table below.

methods.title: Measuring the Speed of a Runner
methods.example: To measure a distance and a time, and use them to calculate the speed of a person running or walking.
steps: Measure and mark out a straight distance, for example 20 metres. Record the exact distance.
One person stands at the finish with the stopwatch; the runner waits at the start.
On the word 'go', the runner runs to the finish while the timer measures the time in seconds.
Record the time. Repeat the run three times and record all three times.
Take the average of your three times to get a reliable time.

methods.title: The Rolling Ball and the Distance-Time Graph
methods.example: To measure how far a rolling ball travels over equal time intervals, and to draw a distance-time graph of its motion.
steps: Roll the ball down a gentle slope so it rolls along the flat ground.
Have a helper call out each second: 'one, two, three...' as the ball rolls.
At each second, quickly mark where the ball is with a stone or chalk.
After the ball stops, measure the distance from the start to each marker and record it.
Make a table of time (0, 1, 2, 3 seconds) against total distance travelled.

workedExamples.title: 1: The density of a stone.
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf finds a smooth stone by the Jubba River. Its mass is 240 g. He lowers it into a jug and the water rises by 80 cm3. What is the density of the stone?
workedExamples.solution: Given: mass m = 240 g; volume V = 80 cm3. Formula: D = m / V. Calculate: D = 240 / 80 = 3. Answer: The density of the stone is 3 g/cm3.

workedExamples.title: 2: The density of cooking oil.
workedExamples.prompt: Halima pours cooking oil into a measuring jug until the volume reads 250 cm3. She weighs it and finds the oil has a mass of 230 g. What is the density of the oil?
workedExamples.solution: Given: m = 230 g; V = 250 cm3. Formula: D = m / V. Calculate: D = 230 / 250 = 0.92. Answer: The density of the cooking oil is 0.92 g/cm3.

workedExamples.title: 3: The density of a block of wood.
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular block of wood measures 10 cm long, 5 cm wide and 4 cm high. Its mass is 120 g. Find its density. (First find the volume, then use the density formula.)
workedExamples.solution: Given: length = 10 cm, width = 5 cm, height = 4 cm; mass m = 120 g. Find the volume first: V = length x width x height = 10 x 5 x 4 = 200 cm3. Formula: D = m / V. Calculate: D = 120 / 200 = 0.6.

workedExamples.title: 4: A block of iron for comparison.
workedExamples.prompt: An iron block has a volume of 50 cm3 and a mass of 395 g. What is its density?
workedExamples.solution: Given: m = 395 g; V = 50 cm3. Formula: D = m / V. Calculate: D = 395 / 50 = 7.9. Answer: The density of the iron is 7.9 g/cm3.

workedExamples.title: 5: Finding mass from density.
workedExamples.prompt: Camel milk has a density of about 1.03 g/cm3. Khadija pours out 500 cm3 of camel milk. What is its mass?
workedExamples.solution: Given: density D = 1.03 g/cm3; volume V = 500 cm3; we must find m. Formula: rearrange D = m / V to get m = D x V. Calculate: m = 1.03 x 500 = 515. Answer: The mass of the camel milk is 515 g.

workedExamples.title: 6: Finding volume from density.
workedExamples.prompt: A piece of aluminium has a mass of 54 g. Aluminium has a density of 2.7 g/cm3. What is the volume of the piece?
workedExamples.solution: Given: m = 54 g; D = 2.7 g/cm3; we must find V. Formula: rearrange D = m / V to get V = m / D. Calculate: V = 54 / 2.7 = 20. Answer: The volume of the aluminium is 20 cm3.

workedExamples.title: 7: A runner's speed.
workedExamples.prompt: Farah runs 100 metres in 20 seconds during a school race. What is his speed?
workedExamples.solution: Given: distance d = 100 m; time t = 20 s. Formula: s = d / t. Calculate: s = 100 / 20 = 5. Answer: Farah's speed is 5 m/s (5 metres per second).

workedExamples.title: 8: A car between two towns.
workedExamples.prompt: A car drives the 150 km from Hargeisa to Berbera in 3 hours. What is its average speed?
workedExamples.solution: Given: distance d = 150 km; time t = 3 h. Formula: s = d / t. Calculate: s = 150 / 3 = 50. Answer: The car's average speed is 50 km/h.

workedExamples.title: 9: A walking camel.
workedExamples.prompt: A camel walks steadily and covers 12 kilometres in 3 hours as it carries goods to market. What is its speed?
workedExamples.solution: Given: d = 12 km; t = 3 h. Formula: s = d / t. Calculate: s = 12 / 3 = 4. Answer: The camel's speed is 4 km/h.

workedExamples.title: 10: A faster runner in metres per second.
workedExamples.prompt: Sagal runs 60 metres in 8 seconds. What is her speed, to one decimal place?
workedExamples.solution: Given: d = 60 m; t = 8 s. Formula: s = d / t. Calculate: s = 60 / 8 = 7.5. Answer: Sagal's speed is 7.5 m/s.

workedExamples.title: 11: Finding distance.
workedExamples.prompt: A bus travels at an average speed of 45 km/h for 4 hours from Nairobi towards the coast. How far does it travel?
workedExamples.solution: Given: speed s = 45 km/h; time t = 4 h; we must find d. Formula: rearrange s = d / t to get d = s x t. Calculate: d = 45 x 4 = 180. Answer: The bus travels 180 km.

workedExamples.title: 12: Finding time.
workedExamples.prompt: Idris must cycle 30 km to the next village. He can cycle at a steady 15 km/h. How long will the journey take?
workedExamples.solution: Given: d = 30 km; s = 15 km/h; we must find t. Formula: rearrange s = d / t to get t = d / s. Calculate: t = 30 / 15 = 2. Answer: The journey will take 2 hours.

practice.prompt: Density is best described as: (a) the mass of an object, (b) the mass packed into each unit of volume, (c) the weight of an object, (d) the size of an object.
practice.answer: 1 (b) the mass packed into each unit of volume Density compares mass with the volume it occupies.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The formula for density is: (a) D = m x V, (b) D = V / m, (c) D = m / V, (d) D = m + V.
practice.answer: 2 (c) D = m / V Density is mass divided by volume.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A common unit of density is: (a) grams (g), (b) cubic centimetres (cm3), (c) grams per cubic centimetre (g/cm3), (d) metres per second (m/s).
practice.answer: 3 (c) grams per cubic centimetre (g/cm3) The unit is built from grams divided by cubic centimetres.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An object with a density of 0.8 g/cm3 placed in water (1 g/cm3) will: (a) sink, (b) float, (c) dissolve, (d) stay in the middle forever.
practice.answer: 4 (b) float An object less dense than water (below 1 g/cm3) floats.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The volume of an irregular stone is best found by: (a) weighing it, (b) measuring with a ruler, (c) the water displacement method, (d) guessing.
practice.answer: 5 (c) the water displacement method The water pushed aside equals the object's volume, so odd shapes can be measured.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A block is 2 cm by 3 cm by 4 cm. Its volume is: (a) 9 cm3, (b) 24 cm3, (c) 12 cm3, (d) 48 cm3.
practice.answer: 6 (b) 24 cm3 Volume of a box = length x width x height = 2 x 3 x 4 = 24 cm3.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The formula for speed is: (a) s = d x t, (b) s = t / d, (c) s = d / t, (d) s = d + t.
practice.answer: 7 (c) s = d / t Speed is distance divided by time.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A common unit of speed is: (a) kilograms (kg), (b) metres per second (m/s), (c) cubic metres (m3), (d) grams (g).
practice.answer: 8 (b) metres per second (m/s) Speed is a distance for each unit of time, such as m/s or km/h.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A car travels 100 km in 2 hours. Its average speed is: (a) 200 km/h, (b) 50 km/h, (c) 102 km/h, (d) 25 km/h.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: On a distance-time graph, a steeper line means the object is moving: (a) slower, (b) faster, (c) backwards, (d) not at all.
practice.answer: 10 (b) faster A steeper line means more distance covered in less time, so a greater speed.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: On a distance-time graph, a flat (horizontal) line means the object is: (a) speeding up, (b) moving fast, (c) stopped, (d) sinking.
practice.answer: 11 (c) stopped A flat line means distance is not changing while time passes, so the object is still.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To find mass when you know density and volume, you use: (a) m = D / V, (b) m = D x V, (c) m = V / D, (d) m = D + V.
practice.answer: 12 (b) m = D x V Rearranging D = m / V gives mass = density x volume.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Measuring the Density of Household Objects
activities.materials: A kitchen scale that measures in grams.; A measuring jug marked in millilitres (remember 1 ml = 1 cm3).; Water to fill the jug part way.; Several small objects that fit in the jug and sink: a stone, a metal spoon, a padlock or key, a marble, a coin.; A ruler, and a notebook and pencil.
steps: Weigh the first object on the kitchen scale and write down its mass in grams.
Pour water into the measuring jug to a level you can read clearly, for example 200 cm3. Record this starting level.
Gently lower the object into the water until it is fully covered. Record the new, higher water level.
Work out the volume of the object by subtracting the starting level from the new level.
Calculate the density using D = mass / volume. Show your working with the four steps.

activities.title: The Float and Sink Test (Density Column)
activities.materials: A tall clear glass or jar.; Water.; Cooking oil (any kind).; A few small test objects: a grape, a piece of cork or dry wood, a small stone, a bottle cap, a piece of orange peel.; A spoon of salt (for the extension).
steps: Half fill the glass with water and let it settle.
Gently pour a layer of cooking oil on top of the water. Watch what happens and record which liquid ends up on top.
One at a time, gently drop in each test object and record where it comes to rest: floating on the oil, floating between oil and water, or sinking to the bottom.
Draw a simple labelled picture of the finished layers and where each object settled.
Record your observations in the table below.

activities.title: Measuring the Speed of a Runner
activities.materials: A tape measure or a long piece of string you can mark in metres.; A clear, flat, safe space outdoors of at least 20 metres, or a long path.; A phone or watch with a stopwatch that counts seconds.; A friend or family member to help time, and a notebook.
steps: Measure and mark out a straight distance, for example 20 metres. Record the exact distance.
One person stands at the finish with the stopwatch; the runner waits at the start.
On the word 'go', the runner runs to the finish while the timer measures the time in seconds.
Record the time. Repeat the run three times and record all three times.
Take the average of your three times to get a reliable time.

activities.title: The Rolling Ball and the Distance-Time Graph
activities.materials: A ball that rolls well, such as an orange, a small ball, or a round tin.; A tape measure or string marked in metres, and some small markers (stones or chalk).; A flat floor or smooth ground and a gentle slope to start the ball rolling.; A phone stopwatch and a helper, plus a notebook and pencil.
steps: Roll the ball down a gentle slope so it rolls along the flat ground.
Have a helper call out each second: 'one, two, three...' as the ball rolls.
At each second, quickly mark where the ball is with a stone or chalk.
After the ball stops, measure the distance from the start to each marker and record it.
Make a table of time (0, 1, 2, 3 seconds) against total distance travelled.

activities.title: Observe: Measuring and Calculating — part 5
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of measuring and calculating — part 5 around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Measuring and Calculating — part 6
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of measuring and calculating — part 6 around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: V = length x width x height
How far an object travels, measured in metres (m) or kilometres (km).
How long something takes, measured in seconds (s), minutes (min) or hours (h).
How fast something moves; s = d / t; units m/s or km/h.
Total distance divided by total time for a whole journey.
A graph of distance (up) against time (along); a steeper line means faster.

vocabulary.meaning: V = length x width x height
How far an object travels, measured in metres (m) or kilometres (km).
vocabulary.example: If a bus leaves Mogadishu and you know the distance and the time, can you work out its speed without ever seeing the speedometer?

vocabulary.meaning: How long something takes, measured in seconds (s), minutes (min) or hours (h).
vocabulary.example: If a bus leaves Mogadishu and you know the distance and the time, can you work out its speed without ever seeing the speedometer?

vocabulary.meaning: How fast something moves; s = d / t; units m/s or km/h.
vocabulary.example: If a bus leaves Mogadishu and you know the distance and the time, can you work out its speed without ever seeing the speedometer?

vocabulary.meaning: Total distance divided by total time for a whole journey.
vocabulary.example: A bus that covers 120 km in 2 hours has an average speed of 60 km/h.

vocabulary.meaning: A graph of distance (up) against time (along); a steeper line means faster.
vocabulary.example: A very powerful way to show a journey is a distance-time graph.

commonMistakes: Thinking heavy always sinks and light always floats
It is density (mass per volume), not total weight, that decides.
Forgetting to find the volume before the density
You need both mass and volume to get density.
Mixing up units of time and distance
Dividing km by minutes gives a wrong number and unit.
Reading a water level from an angle
Looking from the side distorts the reading.
Writing a number with no unit
A number alone is meaningless in science.
One reading may be wrong by chance.

connections.text: This unit builds on your knowledge of matter, particles and states of matter, since density is about how tightly the particles of matter are packed.
Density explains why hot air rises, why some gases and liquids separate into layers, and how materials are chosen for different jobs; speed leads on to the study of forces and motion.
The formulas D = m / V and s = d / t use multiplication, division and rearranging, and distance-time graphs use the plotting skills you learn in maths.
Distances between towns, journeys, coasts and the loading of boats link directly to your geography of East Africa.
Fair weighing and honest measurement in trade, and careful observation of the world, connect this science to daily life and to good character.
Measure the density of five different foods in your kitchen (a potato, an egg, a piece of fruit) and rank them from least to most dense.

fluency.prompt: Density is best described as: (a) the mass of an object, (b) the mass packed into each unit of volume, (c) the weight of an object, (d) the size of an object.
fluency.answer: 1 (b) the mass packed into each unit of volume Density compares mass with the volume it occupies.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 1 (b) the mass packed into each unit of volume Density compares mass with the volume it occupies.

fluency.prompt: The formula for density is: (a) D = m x V, (b) D = V / m, (c) D = m / V, (d) D = m + V.
fluency.answer: 2 (c) D = m / V Density is mass divided by volume.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 2 (c) D = m / V Density is mass divided by volume.

fluency.answer: 3 (c) grams per cubic centimetre (g/cm3) The unit is built from grams divided by cubic centimetres.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 3 (c) grams per cubic centimetre (g/cm3) The unit is built from grams divided by cubic centimetres.

fluency.prompt: An object with a density of 0.8 g/cm3 placed in water (1 g/cm3) will: (a) sink, (b) float, (c) dissolve, (d) stay in the middle forever.
fluency.answer: 4 (b) float An object less dense than water (below 1 g/cm3) floats.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 4 (b) float An object less dense than water (below 1 g/cm3) floats.

fluency.prompt: The volume of an irregular stone is best found by: (a) weighing it, (b) measuring with a ruler, (c) the water displacement method, (d) guessing.
fluency.answer: 5 (c) the water displacement method The water pushed aside equals the object's volume, so odd shapes can be measured.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 5 (c) the water displacement method The water pushed aside equals the object's volume, so odd shapes can be measured.

fluency.prompt: A block is 2 cm by 3 cm by 4 cm. Its volume is: (a) 9 cm3, (b) 24 cm3, (c) 12 cm3, (d) 48 cm3.
fluency.answer: 6 (b) 24 cm3 Volume of a box = length x width x height = 2 x 3 x 4 = 24 cm3.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 6 (b) 24 cm3 Volume of a box = length x width x height = 2 x 3 x 4 = 24 cm3.

fluency.prompt: The formula for speed is: (a) s = d x t, (b) s = t / d, (c) s = d / t, (d) s = d + t.
fluency.answer: 7 (c) s = d / t Speed is distance divided by time.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 7 (c) s = d / t Speed is distance divided by time.

fluency.prompt: A common unit of speed is: (a) kilograms (kg), (b) metres per second (m/s), (c) cubic metres (m3), (d) grams (g).
fluency.answer: 8 (b) metres per second (m/s) Speed is a distance for each unit of time, such as m/s or km/h.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 8 (b) metres per second (m/s) Speed is a distance for each unit of time, such as m/s or km/h.

fluency.prompt: A car travels 100 km in 2 hours. Its average speed is: (a) 200 km/h, (b) 50 km/h, (c) 102 km/h, (d) 25 km/h.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

fluency.prompt: On a distance-time graph, a steeper line means the object is moving: (a) slower, (b) faster, (c) backwards, (d) not at all.
fluency.answer: 10 (b) faster A steeper line means more distance covered in less time, so a greater speed.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 10 (b) faster A steeper line means more distance covered in less time, so a greater speed.

fluency.prompt: On a distance-time graph, a flat (horizontal) line means the object is: (a) speeding up, (b) moving fast, (c) stopped, (d) sinking.
fluency.answer: 11 (c) stopped A flat line means distance is not changing while time passes, so the object is still.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 11 (c) stopped A flat line means distance is not changing while time passes, so the object is still.

fluency.prompt: To find mass when you know density and volume, you use: (a) m = D / V, (b) m = D x V, (c) m = V / D, (d) m = D + V.
fluency.answer: 12 (b) m = D x V Rearranging D = m / V gives mass = density x volume.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 12 (b) m = D x V Rearranging D = m / V gives mass = density x volume.

realProblems.prompt: A river stone from the Jubba has a mass of 360 g and a volume of 120 cm3. Calculate its density. Will it float or sink in water? Show all four steps.
realProblems.answer: Given: m = 360 g, V = 120 cm3. Formula: D = m / V. Calculate: D = 360 / 120 = 3. Answer: 3 g/cm3. Because 3 g/cm3 is greater than water's 1 g/cm3, the stone sinks.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Given: m = 360 g, V = 120 cm3. Formula: D = m / V. Calculate: D = 360 / 120 = 3. Answer: 3 g/cm3. Because 3 g/cm3 is greater than water's 1 g/cm3, the stone sinks.

realProblems.prompt: A jug holds 400 cm3 of camel milk with a mass of 412 g. Calculate the density of the camel milk. Is it more or less dense than water?
realProblems.answer: Given: m = 412 g, V = 400 cm3. Formula: D = m / V. Calculate: D = 412 / 400 = 1.03. Answer: 1.03 g/cm3, which is slightly more dense than water.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Given: m = 412 g, V = 400 cm3. Formula: D = m / V. Calculate: D = 412 / 400 = 1.03. Answer: 1.03 g/cm3, which is slightly more dense than water.

realProblems.prompt: A bus travels the 120 km from Mogadishu to a nearby town in 2 hours. Calculate its average speed. Show all four steps.
realProblems.answer: Given: d = 120 km, t = 2 h. Formula: s = d / t. Calculate: s = 120 / 2 = 60. Answer: 60 km/h.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Given: d = 120 km, t = 2 h. Formula: s = d / t. Calculate: s = 120 / 2 = 60. Answer: 60 km/h.

realProblems.prompt: A camel walks 20 km in 5 hours carrying goods to market. Calculate its speed. Then calculate how far it would walk in 8 hours at the same speed.
realProblems.answer: Speed: s = d / t = 20 / 5 = 4 km/h. Distance in 8 hours: d = s x t = 4 x 8 = 32 km.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Speed: s = d / t = 20 / 5 = 4 km/h. Distance in 8 hours: d = s x t = 4 x 8 = 32 km.

realProblems.prompt: A block of wood is 15 cm by 10 cm by 5 cm and has a mass of 450 g. Calculate its volume, then its density, and state whether it floats in water.
realProblems.answer: Volume: V = 15 x 10 x 5 = 750 cm3. Density: D = m / V = 450 / 750 = 0.6 g/cm3. Because 0.6 g/cm3 is less than 1 g/cm3, the wood floats.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Volume: V = 15 x 10 x 5 = 750 cm3. Density: D = m / V = 450 / 750 = 0.6 g/cm3. Because 0.6 g/cm3 is less than 1 g/cm3, the wood floats.

realProblems.prompt: A metal object has a mass of 240 g. When placed in a jug the water rises from 100 cm3 to 130 cm3. Calculate its density. A book says iron has a density of 7.9 g/cm3 and aluminium 2.7 g/cm3. Which metal is the object most likely made of, and why?
realProblems.answer: Volume = 130 - 100 = 30 cm3. Density = m / V = 240 / 30 = 8 g/cm3. This is very close to iron's 7.9 g/cm3 and far from aluminium's 2.7 g/cm3, so the object is most likely made of iron.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Volume = 130 - 100 = 30 cm3. Density = m / V = 240 / 30 = 8 g/cm3. This is very close to iron's 7.9 g/cm3 and far from aluminium's 2.7 g/cm3, so the object is most likely made of iron.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain how you would find the volume of an irregular object such as a stone.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Density = mass / volume (D = m / V). The units are g/cm3 or kg/m3.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: State the rule that decides whether an object floats or sinks in a liquid.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Lower the object into a measuring jug of water and read the water level before and after. The rise in the water level equals the volume of the object (the displacement method).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between distance and speed.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: An object floats if it is less dense than the liquid, and sinks if it is more dense than the liquid. In water (1 g/cm3), anything below 1 g/cm3 floats and anything above sinks.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe what a flat (horizontal) line on a distance-time graph tells you about the motion.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Speed = distance / time (s = d / t). It can be measured in metres per second (m/s) or kilometres per hour (km/h).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why is it important to write a unit next to every measurement?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Distance is how far an object travels; speed is how far it travels in each unit of time. A large distance can be covered at a low speed if it takes a long time.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why repeating a measurement and taking an average gives a more reliable result.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A flat (horizontal) line means the distance is not changing while time goes on, so the object is stopped (not moving).

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you should be able to do each of the things below. Come back to this list at the end and check yourself honestly.
I can explain what density means as the amount of mass packed into a given volume, and state its units (g/cm3 or kg/m3).
I can measure mass and volume correctly, including finding the volume of an irregular object by displacement of water.
I can calculate density using the relationship density = mass / volume, showing every step of your working.
I can rearrange the density formula to find mass or volume when the other quantities are known.
I can explain floating and sinking by comparing the density of an object with the density of the liquid it is placed in.
I can explain what speed means as the distance travelled in a given time, and state its units (m/s or km/h).
I can calculate speed using the relationship speed = distance / time, showing every step of your working.
